prompt_text:
You are shown a SKELETON view of a python repository: class headers,
inheritance, method signatures, and attributes — but NO method bodies.

Use this signature-level view to detect structural violations of Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP):
- Fat interfaces / bloated classes (many unrelated method groups)
- Clients forced to implement methods they don't need (abstract methods / NotImplementedError / empty overrides)
- Inheritance hierarchies where subclasses alter the contract shape
- Broad A...
raw_response:
{
  "findings": [
    {
      "file_path": "seaborn/_base.py",
      "entity_name": "VectorPlotter",
      "entity_type": "class",
      "line_start": 616,
      "line_end": 1448,
      "principle": "DIP",
      "severity": "high",
      "description": "VectorPlotter defines abstract methods (scale_native, scale_numeric, scale_datetime) that force subclasses to implement specific scaling logic.",
      "reasoning": "The VectorPlotter class acts as a base class that forces its subclasses to implement specific scaling logic (scale_native, scale_numeric, scale_datetime) instead of delegating this behavior to a dedicated Scale abstraction or strategy pattern. This forces subclasses to be aware of and implement low-level scaling details, violating the principle that abstractions should not depend on details."
    },
    {
      "file_path": "seaborn/_core/scales.py",
      "entity_name": "Scale",
      "entity_type": "class",
      "line_start": 55,
      "line_end": 145,
      "principle": "DIP",
      "severity": "medium",
      "description": "Scale base class contains abstract methods that force specific implementation details on all scale types.",
      "reasoning": "The Scale class defines abstract methods like tick, label, and _get_locators. By forcing all subclasses to implement these specific interfaces, it creates a rigid contract where high-level scale abstractions are tightly coupled to the implementation details of Matplotlib's ticker system, rather than being truly decoupled abstractions."
    },
    {
      "file_path": "seaborn/_marks/base.py",
      "entity_name": "Mark",
      "entity_type": "class",
      "line_start": 105,
      "line_end": 229,
      "principle": "DIP",
      "severity": "medium",
      "description": "Mark class forces subclasses to implement _plot and _legend_artist, tying them to specific drawing implementations.",
      "reasoning": "The Mark class forces subclasses to implement _plot and _legend_artist. This forces concrete mark types (Line, Bar, etc.) to handle the low-level rendering logic and Matplotlib integration directly, rather than depending on a higher-level rendering abstraction."
    }
  ]
}

findings.description: VectorPlotter defines abstract methods (scale_native, scale_numeric, scale_datetime) that force subclasses to implement specific scaling logic.
findings.reasoning: The VectorPlotter class acts as a base class that forces its subclasses to implement specific scaling logic (scale_native, scale_numeric, scale_datetime) instead of delegating this behavior to a dedicated Scale abstraction or strategy pattern. This forces subclasses to be aware of and implement low-level scaling details, violating the principle that abstractions should not depend on details.

findings.description: Scale base class contains abstract methods that force specific implementation details on all scale types.
findings.reasoning: The Scale class defines abstract methods like tick, label, and _get_locators. By forcing all subclasses to implement these specific interfaces, it creates a rigid contract where high-level scale abstractions are tightly coupled to the implementation details of Matplotlib's ticker system, rather than being truly decoupled abstractions.

findings.description: Mark class forces subclasses to implement _plot and _legend_artist, tying them to specific drawing implementations.
findings.reasoning: The Mark class forces subclasses to implement _plot and _legend_artist. This forces concrete mark types (Line, Bar, etc.) to handle the low-level rendering logic and Matplotlib integration directly, rather than depending on a higher-level rendering abstraction.
